title: Alzheimer s Disease
authors: Gil D. Rabinovici, Peter A. Ljubenkov, William W. Seeley, Bruce L. Miller

items.title: Prevalence and Economic Impact of Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the leading cause of dementia, accounting for 60-70% of all cases. It significantly impacts healthcare costs, with U.S. expenses projected to reach $360 billion by 2024, averaging about $25,000 per patient.
keyPoints: Approximately 55 million people worldwide live with dementia.
AD accounts for 60–70% of all dementia cases.
Total U.S. healthcare costs related to dementia care are estimated at $360 billion in 2024.
Average cost per AD patient is ~$25,000.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.title: Genetic Risk Factors and Biomarkers for Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: The ε4 allele of the apolipoprotein E (ApoE) gene is a major genetic risk factor for AD. Carrying one ε4 allele increases the risk by two- to threefold in women, while carrying two alleles raises it 10- to 15-fold in both sexes. Advances in biomarkers allow early detection of AD pathologic hallmarks.
keyPoints: Major genetic risk factor: ε4 allele of ApoE gene.
One ε4 allele increases AD risk by two- to threefold in women.
Two ε4 alleles increase AD risk 10- to 15-fold in both sexes.
Biomarkers for Aβ and phosphorylated tau enable early detection.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.title: Progression and Presentation Patterns in Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Alzheimer's Disease (AD) typically begins with memory impairment, progressing to deficits across executive, language, and visuospatial functions. However, 20% of patients present initially with nonmemory complaints such as word-finding difficulties or navigational issues. Other presentations include posterior cortical atrophy syndrome, logopenic aphasia, corticobasal syndrome, and a dysexecutive/behavioral variant.
keyPoints: ~20% of AD patients present with nonmemory complaints
Progression includes deficits in executive, language, visuospatial functions
Early presentations can include posterior cortical atrophy, logopenic aphasia, corticobasal syndrome
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Stages of Alzheimer's Disease and Associated Cognitive Decline
items.content: In early AD, memory loss may be unrecognized or attributed to aging. The term 'mild cognitive impairment' (MCI) is used when memory loss becomes noticeable, with ~50% progressing to dementia over 4 years (~12% per year). Preclinical AD involves biomarker evidence of amyloid pathology without symptoms, potentially preceding clinical symptoms by 20+ years.
keyPoints: Early stage: Memory loss may be unrecognized or ascribed to aging
~50% of MCI patients progress to dementia over 4 years (~12% per year)
Preclinical AD can precede symptoms by 20+ years
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Seizures as a Feature in Alzheimer's Disease Progression
items.content: Emerging evidence suggests that partial and generalized seizures may herald AD, occurring even before dementia onset. This is particularly noted in younger patients and those with autosomal dominant AD-causing mutations.
keyPoints: Partial and generalized seizures can precede dementia onset
More common in younger patients and those with autosomal dominant AD mutations
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: When to Suspect Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Alzheimer's disease is suggested by a slowly progressive decline in memory and orientation, normal systemic laboratory tests, and MRI or CT scans showing distributed or posteriorly predominant cortical and hippocampal atrophy.
keyPoints: Slowly progressive decline in memory and orientation
Normal results on systemic laboratory tests
MRI/CT scan shows distributed or posteriorly predominant cortical and hippocampal atrophy
Clinical diagnosis of AD confirmed at autopsy ~70–80% of the time
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis Clues for Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Certain clinical features can help differentiate Alzheimer's disease from other dementias. For instance, early prominent gait disturbance with mild memory loss suggests vascular dementia or NPH; resting tremor with stooped posture and bradykinesia suggest PD or DLB.
keyPoints: Early prominent gait disturbance with mild memory loss: consider vascular dementia or NPH
Resting tremor, stooped posture, bradykinesia, masked facies: suggest PD or DLB
Dementia after established PD diagnosis usually indicates PDD, but may show AD and LBD at autopsy
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Imaging in Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Structural imaging like CT or MRI is used to exclude other disorders and distinguish AD from other degenerative diseases. Functional imaging reveals hypometabolism in the posterior temporal-parietal cortex, while amyloid PET confirms Aβ plaques presence.
keyPoints: CT/MRI: no specific pattern for AD early on, but shows distributed cortical atrophy as disease progresses
Functional imaging (FDG-PET): reveals hypometabolism in the posterior temporal-parietal cortex
Amyloid PET: confirms Aβ plaques presence; negative scan makes AD unlikely
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Tau PET Radiotracers in AD Diagnosis
items.content: Tau PET radiotracers, such as $[^{18}F]$flortaucipir, are approved for clinical use to estimate tau tangle density and distribution in patients with cognitive impairment. They follow Braak neuropathologic staging but have limited sensitivity, making them less useful in early-stage neurofibrillary pathology or non-AD tauopathies.
keyPoints: $[^{18}F]$flortaucipir is approved for clinical use in the U.S.
Tau PET binding pattern aligns with Braak staging
Limited sensitivity means negative Tau PET does not rule out early neurofibrillary pathology (Braak stages I–III)
Limited utility in detecting tau in non-AD tauopathies like PSP, CBD, CTE
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: CSF Biomarkers for AD
items.content: In Alzheimer's Disease, CSF reductions in $A eta_{42}$ levels and the $A eta_{42}/A eta_{40}$ ratio correlate with amyloid deposition. Increases in phosphorylated tau (p-Tau181 or p-Tau217) indicate AD-related tau changes. The p-Tau181/$A eta_{42}$ ratio shows higher agreement with amyloid PET results.
keyPoints: $A eta_{42}$ levels and $A eta_{42}/A eta_{40}$ ratio correlate with amyloid deposition
Increases in phosphorylated tau (p-Tau181 or p-Tau217) detect AD-related tau changes
The p-Tau181/$A eta_{42}$ ratio shows higher agreement with amyloid PET results
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Plasma Biomarkers in AD Diagnosis
items.content: Recent advances have introduced plasma measurements of $\mathring{A} eta$ and phosphorylated tau, which correlate with amyloid PET scans. These biomarkers can help establish an AD diagnosis when used alongside clinical evaluation, though medical comorbidities may affect their accuracy.
keyPoints: Plasma assays for $\mathring{A} eta_{42}^{4}/A eta_{40}$ and phosphorylated tau (p-Tau181, p-Tau217) show high concordance with amyloid PET
Plasma biomarkers can establish AD diagnosis in certain cases
Medical comorbidities may impact blood concentrations of $\mathring{A} eta$ and p-Tau
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Age-Related Prevalence of Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: The prevalence of Alzheimer's disease increases significantly with age, highlighting the importance of considering age as a primary risk factor in diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: ~10% of people over age 65 have AD
3% of people age 65–74 have AD
17% of people age 75–84 have AD
32% of people age 85 and older have AD
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Genetic and Environmental Risk Factors for Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: A positive family history, particularly involving the ApoE ε4 allele, significantly contributes to the genetic risk of developing AD. Additionally, environmental factors such as traumatic brain injury and vascular disease can influence the onset and progression.
keyPoints: Positive family history suggests a genetic contribution
ApoE ε4 risk allele is a significant genetic factor
Autosomal dominant inheritance occurs in 1–2% of patients
History of mild-to-severe traumatic brain injury increases AD risk
Vascular disease, especially stroke, lowers the threshold for clinical expression of AD
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Key Pathological Features of Alzheimer's Disease (AD)
items.content: Alzheimer's disease pathology is characterized by specific brain regions experiencing early and severe degeneration, including the medial temporal lobe and nucleus basalis of Meynert. The hallmark microscopic findings are neuritic plaques and neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs), which accumulate significantly in AD compared to normal aging.
keyPoints: Early degeneration occurs in medial temporal lobe, inferolateral temporal cortex, and nucleus basalis of Meynert.
Characteristic microscopic findings: neuritic plaques and NFTs.
Neuritic plaques contain a core of amyloid with surrounding proteins including ApoE and α-antichymotrypsin.
NFTs are composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein disrupting microtubule stability.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATHOLOGY

items.title: Role of Amyloid and Tau in Alzheimer's Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Amyloid plaques, primarily consisting of Aβ peptides derived from APP cleavage by β and γ secretases, are central to AD pathology. Soluble amyloid oligomers may be the early toxic species causing cellular dysfunction. Hyperphosphorylated tau protein forms NFTs, disrupting axonal transport and contributing to neuronal degeneration.
keyPoints: Aβ peptides (39–42 amino acids) derived from APP cleavage by β and γ secretases.
Soluble amyloid oligomers may be the early toxic species in AD.
Hyperphosphorylated tau disrupts microtubule binding, leading to NFT formation.
Tau-lowering therapies show promise in rodent models with Aβ₄₂ accumulation.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATHOLOGY

items.title: Role of APP Gene in Alzheimer's Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: The APP gene on chromosome 21 is crucial for the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease, particularly in individuals with trisomy 21. The extra dose of this gene leads to increased cerebral amyloid production, causing typical neuropathologic hallmarks of AD and progressive dementia if they survive beyond age 40.
keyPoints: APP gene on chromosome 21 causes excess cerebral amyloid production
Adults with Down's syndrome develop AD neuropathology if they live past 40 years
Point mutations in APP linked to early-onset familial AD (FAD)
Single-gene autosomal dominant transmission of AD is rare but significant
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Presenilin Gene Mutations on Early-Onset Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Mutations in presenilin genes, particularly PSEN-1 and PSEN-2, are major genetic factors for early-onset familial AD. PSEN-1 mutations are more common and lead to an earlier onset (mean 45 years) with a rapid progression (6–7 years), while PSEN-2 mutations have a later onset (mean 53 years) and longer duration (11 years).
keyPoints: PSEN-1 on chromosome 14, PSEN-2 on chromosome 1
Over 100 different PSEN-1 mutations identified across ethnicities
PSEN-1 mutations account for 40–70% of early-age-of-onset FAD cases
Elevated CSF levels of Aβ42 in presenilin mutation carriers before symptom onset
Clinical genetic testing advised with counseling for early-onset FAD
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Apoε4 Allele's Role in Alzheimer's Disease Risk
items.content: The Apoε4 allele on chromosome 19 significantly increases the risk of developing Alzheimer's disease (AD). It is associated with a higher prevalence among AD patients compared to controls and influences age of onset.
keyPoints: Apoε4 allele confers increased risk of AD in both sporadic and familial forms.
24–30% of nondemented white population have at least one ε4 allele, while ~2% are ε4/ε4 homozygotes.
40–65% of patients with AD have at least one ε4 allele.
Risk is approximately three times higher in single ε4 allele carriers and 10–15 times higher in ε4 homozygotes compared to noncarriers.
Apoε4 allele associated with ~10-year earlier age of onset for AD, strongest between ages 60-85.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Differential Risk and Protective Factors in Apoε Alleles
items.content: The presence or absence of specific Apoε alleles influences the risk and onset of Alzheimer's disease, with ε4 increasing risk and ε2 potentially reducing it.
keyPoints: Apoε4 increases AD risk; ε2 may reduce it.
Risk varies by racial/ethnic background: increased in East Asians, decreased in African Americans and Hispanics compared to whites.
Christchurch mutation in Apoε3 may be protective against tau aggregation despite amyloid plaques.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Genetic Testing for Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Apoε4 testing is controversial in AD diagnosis due to its predictive limitations, but it is recommended before anti-Aβ monoclonal antibody treatment.
keyPoints: Use of Apoε4 testing remains controversial; many ε4 carriers never develop dementia.
Recommended for patients evaluated for anti-Aβ monoclonal antibodies to assess risk of adverse effects.
Genotyping available on some direct-to-consumer platforms.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Cholinesterase Inhibitors in Alzheimer's: Mechanism and Impact on Cognitive Decline
items.content: Cholinesterase inhibitors, including donepezil, rivastigmine, and galantamine, work by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, increasing cerebral acetylcholine levels. They modestly improve cognitive function and slow decline in Alzheimer's disease (AD) patients.
keyPoints: Donepezil target dose: 10 mg daily
Rivastigmine target dose: 6 mg twice daily or 9.5-mg patch daily
Galantamine target dose: 24 mg daily, extended-release
Cholinesterase inhibitors improve caregiver ratings and slow cognitive decline over up to 3 years.
Average MMSE score maintenance for ~1 year with anticholinesterase inhibitor vs. 2-3 point decline in placebo-treated patients.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | NEUROTRANSMITTER-BASED THERAPIES

items.title: Role of Memantine in Moderate to Severe Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Memantine, an NMDA receptor antagonist, is used alongside cholinesterase inhibitors or alone in moderate-to-severe AD. It slows cognitive deterioration and reduces caregiver burden but is not approved for mild AD.
keyPoints: Memantine target dose: 10 mg twice daily
Effective in slowing cognitive decline and reducing caregiver burden in moderate to severe AD.
Not approved for use in mild AD or MCI due to lack of efficacy proven in these stages.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | NEUROTRANSMITTER-BASED THERAPIES

items.title: FDA-approved antiamyloid monoclonal antibodies for early AD
items.content: Lecanemab and donanemab are FDA-approved for treating early-stage Alzheimer's disease by targeting specific Aβ epitopes. They have shown clinical efficacy in slowing cognitive decline, as evidenced by phase 3 trials.
keyPoints: Lecanemab targets Aβ protofibrils; donanemab targets pyroglutamate form of Aβ.
FDA-approved based on evidence from double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled clinical trials.
Lecanemab: 27% slowing in CDR-SB over 18 months (Clarity-AD trial).
Donanemab: 22% slowing on iADRS and 28% on CDR-SB over 76 weeks (TRAILBLAZER-ALZ2 trial).
Treatment is restricted to patients with MCI or mild dementia with confirmed Aβ pathology.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.title: Clinical efficacy and biomarker-guided treatment duration
items.content: Antiamyloid therapies show greater benefit in patients with lower tau PET at baseline, suggesting early intervention may be more effective. Treatment duration can be guided by amyloid PET clearance.
keyPoints: Patients with low-medium tau PET showed greatest clinical benefit (35% slowing on iADRS and 36% on CDR-SB).
Treatment duration can be titrated based on amyloid PET response, potentially limiting therapy to necessary periods.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.title: Managing risks of antiamyloid antibody therapy
items.content: Antiamyloid antibodies are associated with ARIA, which includes edema and hemorrhage. Risk is higher in Apo ε4 homozygotes, necessitating genotyping before treatment.
keyPoints: ARIA-E rates: 13% for lecanemab, 24% for donanemab; ARIA-H rates: 17% for lecanemab, 31% for donanemab.
Approximately 75% of ARIA cases are asymptomatic.
Severe symptoms include seizures and stroke-like episodes.
Risk increases with Apo ε4 allele copies; genotyping is recommended.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.title: Contraindications and Monitoring for Antiamyloid Antibody Therapy
items.content: Antiamyloid antibody therapy, such as lecanemab or donanemab, is contraindicated in patients with uncontrolled hypertension, extensive CAA (>4 brain microhemorrhages or ≥1 region of cortical siderosis), and those on anticoagulants due to increased risk of ARIA. Patients should be monitored for ARIA using MRI during the first 6–12 months of treatment.
keyPoints: Contraindications include uncontrolled hypertension, extensive CAA (>4 brain microhemorrhages or ≥1 region of cortical siderosis), and anticoagulant use.
Patients on single antiplatelet agents are not at higher risk for ARIA.
MRI monitoring is recommended during the first 6–12 months of treatment with T2/FLAIR and GRE/SWI sequences.
Urgent MRI should be performed if ARIA is clinically suspected based on symptoms.
Symptomatic or radiographically moderate to severe ARIA requires temporary suspension of treatment until resolution.
Permanent cessation recommended for severe symptoms, recurrence of ARIA more than twice, >10 additional microhemorrhages, or multiple areas of superficial siderosis.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 2)

items.title: Safety Considerations and Patient Selection for Antiamyloid Antibody Therapy
items.content: Antiamyloid antibody therapy is restricted to patients with biomarker-confirmed Aβ pathology, early symptomatic disease, and no expected need for anticoagulant therapy. ApoE allele status should be known due to associated ARIA risk, especially in Apoε4 homozygous patients.
keyPoints: Therapy is restricted to patients with biomarker-confirmed Aβ pathology and early symptomatic disease (e.g., MCI or mild AD dementia).
Patients should not be expected to require anticoagulant therapy.
ApoE allele status must be known; Apoε4 homozygous patients have higher ARIA risk.
Baseline brain MRI is scrutinized for disqualifying lesions that may increase risk of ARIA and intracerebral hemorrhage.
Regular MRI safety screening is required to rule out ARIA.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.title: Importance of Lifestyle Modifications in Alzheimer's Disease Management
items.content: Encouraging lifestyle modifications can be protective against aging and neurodegeneration in AD patients. These include physical, cognitive, social activities, vascular risk factor modification, healthy sleep habits, and dietary changes like the Mediterranean or DASH diets.
keyPoints: Lifestyle modifications may protect against aging and neurodegeneration.
Activities: Physical, cognitive, social; Vascular risk factor modification; Healthy sleep habits; Dietary modifications (Mediterranean or DASH Diets).
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.title: Managing Neuropsychiatric Symptoms in Alzheimer's Disease with Medications
items.content: Atypical antipsychotics, such as risperidone, quetiapine, and olanzapine, are used to treat agitation, insomnia, hallucinations, and belligerence in AD. Brexpiprazole is FDA-approved for treating agitation based on a 12-week trial.
keyPoints: Atypical antipsychotics: Risperidone, quetiapine, olanzapine used at low doses.
Brexpiprazole approved for agitation in AD (based on a 12-week trial).
FDA warning: Antipsychotics carry risks including sleep, gait, cardiovascular complications.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.title: Evaluating the Efficacy of Supplements and Unproven Treatments in AD
items.content: Commonly used supplements like estrogen, statins, vitamin E, and ginkgo biloba have not shown efficacy in controlled trials for preventing AD. Anecdotal treatments such as anti-interferon infusions and stem cell therapies lack scientific evidence and pose significant harm risks.
keyPoints: Supplements: Estrogen, statins, vitamin E, ginkgo biloba show no efficacy in randomized trials.
Anecdotal treatments (e.g., anti-interferon, stem cells) lack evidence and risk harm.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.title: Role of Biomarkers in AD Clinical Trials
items.content: The inclusion of biomarker evidence has transformed the design of Alzheimer's Disease (AD) clinical trials. Biomarkers such as Aβ and tau are now used to assess target engagement and modification of disease pathophysiology, exemplified by pivotal trials leading to the approval of lecanemab and donanemab.
keyPoints: Biomarker evidence required for trial inclusion in AD.
Used to assess target engagement (e.g., changes in Aβ biomarkers) or modification of disease pathophysiology (e.g., changes in tau biomarkers).
Pivotal trials for lecanemab and donanemab exemplify this approach.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES

items.title: Challenges with Secretase Inhibitors in AD Therapy
items.content: Oral drugs inhibiting β and γ secretase aimed to reduce Aβ42 cleavage from APP, showing promise in animal models but failing in human trials. Clinical efficacy was not demonstrated, and some trials noted transient cognitive worsening upon treatment.
keyPoints: Oral drugs inhibit β and γ secretase to reduce Aβ42 cleavage.
Promising results in AD transgenic mice but failed in placebo-controlled human trials.
Some trials reported significant, albeit transient, cognitive worsening with β secretase inhibitors.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES

items.title: Distinguishing Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease (CJD) from Other Rapidly Progressive Dementias
items.content: CJD is a rare, rapidly progressive neurodegenerative disorder with distinct clinical and imaging features that help differentiate it from other dementias like Alzheimer's disease. Key diagnostic tools include EEG showing markedly abnormal periodic complexes and MRI revealing cortical ribboning and basal ganglia hyperintensities.
keyPoints: CJD prevalence is ~1 per million
Rapid progression with death <1 year after symptom onset
EEG shows markedly abnormal periodic complexes
MRI reveals cortical ribboning and basal ganglia hyperintensities
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Features and Diagnosis of Huntington's Disease (HD)
items.content: Huntington's disease is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by chorea, behavioral disturbances, and executive impairment. Symptoms typically begin in the fourth or fifth decade but can vary widely. Depression and social withdrawal are common, with memory often spared until late stages.
keyPoints: Autosomal dominant inheritance
Symptoms start in the fourth or fifth decade
Chorea, behavioral disturbance, executive impairment
Memory typically not impaired until late disease
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH) and Differentiating from Other Dementias
items.content: NPH is characterized by a triad of gait disturbance, dementia with executive impairment, and urinary urgency or incontinence. Neuroimaging shows enlarged lateral ventricles without significant cortical atrophy. It differs from other dementias like AD due to the early prominent gait disturbance.
keyPoints: Clinical triad: abnormal gait, mild-moderate dementia, urinary symptoms
MRI shows enlarged lateral ventricles with little cortical atrophy
Distinguishing feature: early and prominent gait disturbance without cortical atrophy
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosing Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus vs Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Distinguishing between normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) and Alzheimer's disease (AD) can be challenging, especially when a patient presents with gait impairment. MRI findings such as hippocampal atrophy suggest AD, while NPH is indicated by a 'magnetic' gait and truncal sway. However, the absence of hydrocephalus on imaging should prevent an NPH diagnosis.
keyPoints: Hippocampal atrophy on MRI favors AD diagnosis.
Characteristic 'magnetic' gait with external hip rotation, low foot clearance, short strides, and prominent truncal sway or instability suggests NPH.
Diagnosis of NPH should be avoided without hydrocephalus detected on imaging studies.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.title: Predicting Response to Ventricular Shunting in NPH
items.content: In patients with normal pressure hydrocephalus, ventricular shunting may improve symptoms. However, the presence of positive CSF AD biomarkers or amyloid PET imaging is associated with a lower likelihood of response. Careful patient selection for shunting is crucial due to potential complications.
keyPoints: 30–50% of patients diagnosed with NPH will improve with ventricular shunting.
Positive CSF AD biomarkers or amyloid PET are linked to a reduced chance of improvement post-shunt.
Complications from shunting include subdural hematoma, infection, and shunt failure.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.title: Thiamine Deficiency and Its Impact on Cognitive Function
items.content: Thiamine deficiency can lead to Wernicke's encephalopathy, characterized by confusion, ataxia, and diplopia. If untreated, it may progress to Korsakoff's syndrome, an amnestic condition with confabulation. Prompt thiamine administration is crucial for reversal.
keyPoints: Wernicke's encephalopathy presents with confusion, ataxia, and diplopia due to periventricular midline structure inflammation.
Korsakoff's syndrome results from prolonged untreated thiamine deficiency, leading to memory loss and confabulation.
Prompt administration of parenteral thiamine (100 mg intravenously for 3 days) may reverse Wernicke's encephalopathy.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency as a Cause of Dementia
items.content: Vitamin B12 deficiency can lead to dementia due to damage to myelinated axons. Neurological symptoms include spinal cord syndrome, neuropathy, and dementia. The mechanism may involve deficiencies in S-adenosyl methionine or accumulation of abnormal substrates for fatty acid synthesis.
keyPoints: Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause dementia by damaging myelinated axons.
Neurological symptoms include loss of vibration/position sense, hyperactive tendon reflexes with Babinski signs, and sensory loss with depressed tendon reflexes.
Mechanism may involve S-adenosyl methionine deficiency or accumulation of methylmalonate, homocysteine, and propionate.
Treatment involves parenteral vitamin B12 (1000 mcg intramuscularly daily for a week, weekly for a month, monthly for life in pernicious anemia).
Neurologic sequelae may occur without hematologic manifestations; do not rely on CBC or blood smear.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.title: Chronic CNS Infections and Dementia
items.content: Chronic CNS infections, such as those causing chronic meningitis, can lead to dementia. Symptoms include headache, meningismus, cranial neuropathy, radiculopathy, psychomotor retardation, apathy, and impaired memory.
keyPoints: Chronic CNS infections may produce dementing illness, especially with chronic meningitis.
Symptoms: headache, meningismus, cranial neuropathy, radiculopathy, psychomotor retardation, apathy, impaired memory.
20-30% of advanced HIV patients become demented; consider secondary opportunistic infections or direct CNS neuron infection by HIV.
Neurosyphilis can cause dementia with pleocytosis, increased protein, and positive VDRL test in CSF.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.title: Autoimmune Etiologies of Dementia
items.content: Autoimmune syndromes can cause dementia. Limbic encephalitis, often paraneoplastic and associated with small-cell lung cancer or ovarian teratoma, presents with confusion, agitation, seizures, poor memory, and emotional changes.
keyPoints: Limbic encephalitis is an autoimmune syndrome of dementia linked to occult carcinoma (often small-cell lung cancer).
Symptoms include confusion, agitation, seizures, poor memory, emotional changes, and frank dementia.
Paraneoplastic encephalitis with NMDA receptor antibodies presents as progressive psychiatric disorder with memory loss and seizures.
Autoimmune etiologies also involve LGI1, Caspr2, and AMPA receptor antibodies.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.title: CNS Vasculitis and Its Mimics
items.content: Central Nervous System (CNS) granulomatous angiitis can cause chronic encephalopathy with symptoms such as confusion, disorientation, and clouding of consciousness. It may present with headache, strokes, or cranial neuropathies. Brain imaging might be normal or nonspecifically abnormal, while CSF analysis shows mild pleocytosis or protein elevation. Cerebral angiography can reveal multifocal stenoses but is not specific as it may mimic atherosclerosis or other vascular diseases.
keyPoints: Symptoms: confusion, disorientation, clouding of consciousness, headache, strokes, cranial neuropathies
Brain imaging: normal or nonspecifically abnormal
CSF analysis: mild pleocytosis or protein elevation
Cerebral angiography: multifocal stenoses in medium-caliber vessels; not specific
Mimics include atherosclerosis, infection, and other vascular diseases
Prognosis can be poor but may remit spontaneously; some respond to glucocorticoids or chemotherapy
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.title: Chronic Metal Exposure as a Rare Cause of Dementia
items.content: Dementia can result from chronic exposure to metals like lead, mercury, and arsenic. Lead poisoning may present with fatigue, depression, confusion, abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, gray gum lines, and basophilic stippling of red blood cells. Mercury poisoning is characterized by dementia, peripheral neuropathy, ataxia, tremulousness, cerebellar intention tremor, or choreoathetosis. Arsenic intoxication includes nausea, weight loss, peripheral neuropathy, skin pigmentation, scaling, and Mees' lines on fingernails. Chelation therapy is the treatment for these conditions.
keyPoints: Lead poisoning: fatigue, depression, confusion, abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, gray gum lines, basophilic stippling
Mercury poisoning: dementia, peripheral neuropathy, ataxia, tremulousness, cerebellar intention tremor or choreoathetosis
Arsenic intoxication: nausea, weight loss, peripheral neuropathy, skin pigmentation and scaling, Mees' lines
Treatment for all: chelation therapy (e.g., EDTA for lead, BAL for mercury)
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.title: Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy in Athletes
items.content: Chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) is a dementia resulting from recurrent head trauma, often seen in athletes. Symptoms include personality changes, social instability, explosive rage, paranoia, delusions, memory loss, parkinsonian signs, ataxia, or intention tremor. Autopsy findings show tau-immunoreactive neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) and TDP-43 inclusions, with neuron loss in the substantia nigra being variable.
keyPoints: Symptoms: personality change, social instability, explosive rage, paranoia, delusions, memory loss, parkinsonian signs, ataxia or intention tremor
Autopsy findings: tau-immunoreactive NFTs, TDP-43 inclusions, variable neuron loss in substantia nigra
Associated with contact sports athletes beyond boxers
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.question: What is the estimated percentage of dementia cases attributed to Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: Alzheimer's disease contributes to an estimated 60–70% of all dementia cases.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.question: Which brain regions are initially affected by atrophy in typical amnestic Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: In typical amnestic AD, brain atrophy begins in the medial temporal lobes.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.question: What is the major genetic risk factor for Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: The major genetic risk factor for AD is the ε4 allele of the apolipoprotein E (ApoE) gene.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.question: Which cognitive function is typically affected first in Alzheimer's Disease (AD)?
items.explanation: The cognitive changes of AD begin with memory impairment.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What term is used for self-perceived worsening in memory or other cognitive abilities that may not be noticeable to others?
items.explanation: The term subjective cognitive decline refers to self-perceived worsening in memory or other cognitive abilities.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: In which stage of Alzheimer's Disease do patients typically require daily supervision?
items.explanation: In the middle stages, the patient is unable to work and requires daily supervision.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What symptom is prevalent in the late stages of Alzheimer's Disease?
items.explanation: In the late stages, delusions are prevalent and usually simple.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which imaging technique is used to confirm the presence of neuritic and diffuse Aβ plaques in Alzheimer's Disease?
items.explanation: Amyloid PET imaging is used to confirm the presence of neuritic and diffuse Aβ plaques throughout the neocortex.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What does a negative amyloid PET scan in a patient with dementia suggest?
items.explanation: A negative amyloid PET scan makes an AD diagnosis unlikely.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which condition should be considered when a patient presents with early prominent gait disturbance and mild memory loss?
options: Vascular dementia or normal pressure hydrocephalus
items.explanation: Early prominent gait disturbance with only mild memory loss suggests vascular dementia or, rarely, normal pressure hydrocephalus.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What is the main purpose of structural neuroimaging in Alzheimer's Disease?
options: To show specific patterns of AD
items.explanation: The main purpose of structural imaging is to exclude other disorders, such as primary and secondary neoplasms.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which tau PET radiotracer is approved for clinical use in the United States to estimate the density and distribution of aggregated tau neurofibrillary tangles?
items.explanation: [18F]Flortaucipir is approved for clinical use in the United States.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What does a completely negative tau PET scan not rule out?
items.explanation: A completely negative tau PET does not rule out early neurofibrillary pathology (Braak stages I–III).
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: Which biomarker assay shows higher agreement with amyloid PET results or AD neuropathology than any single CSF analyte?
items.explanation: Decreases in the Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio show higher agreement with amyloid PET results or AD neuropathology.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is a limitation of tau PET radiotracers developed for detecting AD-related tau aggregation?
options: Inability to detect any neurofibrillary tangles
items.explanation: Tau PET radiotracers have limited utility in detecting aggregated tau in non-AD tauopathies.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of people aged 85 and older have Alzheimer's disease in the United States?
items.explanation: Approximately 32% of individuals aged 85 and older are affected by Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which genetic factor is most commonly associated with a familial contribution to Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: The apolipoprotein E (ApoE) ε4 risk allele is commonly associated with a genetic contribution to Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which factor increases the risk of Alzheimer's disease threefold?
items.explanation: Diabetes is associated with a threefold increase in the risk of developing Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which brain region is typically the earliest and most severely affected by degeneration in Alzheimer's Disease (AD)?
options: Medial temporal lobe, including entorhinal/perirhinal cortex and hippocampus
items.explanation: The medial temporal lobe is the earliest and most severely affected region in AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATHOLOGY

items.question: What are the characteristic microscopic findings in Alzheimer's Disease?
items.explanation: Neuritic plaques and neurofibrillary tangles are the characteristic microscopic findings in AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATHOLOGY

items.question: What process is responsible for tau spreading in Alzheimer's Disease according to one theory?
options: Prion-like templating of abnormal tau conformations
items.explanation: Abnormal conformations of tau induce misfolding into pathologic forms, a prion-like templating process.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which gene is associated with the production of excess cerebral amyloid in adults with trisomy 21?
items.explanation: The extra dose of the APP gene on chromosome 21 is responsible for excess cerebral amyloid production in adults with trisomy 21.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which presenilin gene mutation is most commonly associated with early-age-of-onset familial AD?
items.explanation: Mutations in PSEN-1 are the most common cause of early-age-of-onset familial AD, representing 40–70% of all cases.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which presenilin gene was first identified in a group of American families with Volga German ethnic background?
items.explanation: A mutation in the PSEN-2 gene was first found in a group of American families with Volga German ethnic background.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which allele of the Apoε gene is associated with an increased risk of Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: The Apoε4 allele confers increased risk of AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is the approximate increase in risk for Alzheimer's disease associated with a single ε4 allele compared to noncarriers?
items.explanation: The increased risk of AD associated with a single ε4 allele is approximately three times higher than in ε4 noncarriers.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which racial or ethnic group has a decreased risk of Alzheimer's disease compared to whites when carrying the Apoε4 allele?
items.explanation: The risk of AD in Apo ε4 carriers is decreased in African Americans and Hispanics compared to whites.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which gene variant is associated with a strong increase in the likelihood of developing clinical Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: TREM2 risk alleles are rare but have strong effects, with odds ratios estimated at 3–4 for developing clinical AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following medications is approved by the FDA for use in moderate-to-severe Alzheimer's disease and acts by blocking overexcited NMDA glutamate receptors?
items.explanation: Memantine is approved for moderate-to-severe AD and acts by blocking NMDA glutamate receptors.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | NEUROTRANSMITTER-BASED THERAPIES

items.question: What is the primary pharmacologic action of donepezil, rivastigmine, and galantamine in Alzheimer's disease treatment?
options: Increase in cerebral acetylcholine levels by inhibiting cholinesterases
items.explanation: Donepezil, rivastigmine, and galantamine inhibit cholinesterases, increasing cerebral acetylcholine levels.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | NEUROTRANSMITTER-BASED THERAPIES

items.question: Which of the following is a common side effect associated with memantine in Alzheimer's disease treatment?
items.explanation: Memantine can cause constipation as a side effect.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | NEUROTRANSMITTER-BASED THERAPIES

items.question: Which of the following antiamyloid monoclonal antibodies is approved for targeting Aβ protofibrils in early-stage Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: Lecanemab targets Aβ protofibrils and is approved for early-stage Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.question: What was the primary outcome measure in the Clarity-AD trial of lecanemab?
options: Clinical Dementia Rating Scale sum of boxes score
items.explanation: The primary outcome measure in the Clarity-AD trial was the Clinical Dementia Rating Scale sum of boxes score.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.question: Which antiamyloid antibody therapy showed a 35% slowing on the integrated Alzheimer Disease Rating Scale in patients with low-medium tau PET at trial entry?
items.explanation: Donanemab showed a 35% slowing on the integrated Alzheimer Disease Rating Scale in patients with low-medium tau PET at trial entry.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an antiamyloid antibody that received FDA approval for Alzheimer's disease treatment?
items.explanation: Gantenerumab did not receive FDA approval for Alzheimer's disease treatment.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a contraindication for antiamyloid antibodies due to increased ARIA risk?
options: Limited data on safety with anticoagulants
items.explanation: Uncontrolled hypertension is a contraindication for antiamyloid antibodies due to increased ARIA risk.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 2)

items.question: What imaging sequences are recommended during the first 6–12 months of treatment with lecanemab or donanemab?
items.explanation: T2/FLAIR and GRE/SWI sequences are recommended during the first 6–12 months of treatment with lecanemab or donanemab.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 2)

items.question: What should be done if a patient develops symptomatic or radiographically moderate to severe ARIA?
options: Temporarily suspend treatment until resolution of symptoms
items.explanation: Symptomatic or radiographically moderate to severe ARIA should lead to temporary suspension of treatment until ARIA-E resolves and ARIA-H stabilizes.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.question: Which dietary modification is suggested as potentially protective against aging and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease patients?
items.explanation: The Mediterranean Diet, along with the Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH) Diets, are mentioned as potentially protective against aging and neurodegeneration.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.question: Which medication has FDA approval for the treatment of agitation in Alzheimer's disease?
items.explanation: Brexpiprazole is the only drug to receive FDA approval for treatment of agitation in Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.question: What type of medications should be avoided due to their strong anticholinergic effects in Alzheimer's patients?
items.explanation: Medications with strong anticholinergic effects, such as prescription and over-the-counter sleep aids (e.g., diphenhydramine), should be avoided.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.question: Which class of medications is commonly used for treating mild to moderate depression in early Alzheimer's disease due to low anticholinergic side effects?
items.explanation: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are commonly used due to their low anticholinergic side effects.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: Which biomarkers are commonly used in AD clinical trials to assess target engagement in antiamyloid therapies?
items.explanation: Changes in Aβ biomarkers are assessed for target engagement in antiamyloid trials.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES

items.question: What was a significant safety concern identified during the first $A eta_{42}$ vaccine trial?
items.explanation: The first $A eta_{42}$ vaccine trial was aborted due to meningoencephalitis in a minority of patients.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES

items.question: Which type of drugs failed to show clinical efficacy in placebo-controlled trials for AD?
items.explanation: Oral drugs that inhibit $\beta$ and $\gamma$ secretase failed to show clinical efficacy in placebo-controlled trials.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES

items.question: Which imaging feature is diagnostic for Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD)?
options: Enlarged lateral ventricles with little or no cortical atrophy
Markedly abnormal periodic complexes on EEG and cortical ribboning
items.explanation: CJD is characterized by markedly abnormal periodic complexes on EEG and cortical ribboning.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.question: What is a common early symptom in Huntington's disease?
items.explanation: Huntington's disease often presents with chorea as an early symptom.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is associated with a patent aqueduct of Sylvius and enlarged lateral ventricles?
items.explanation: NPH is characterized by a patent aqueduct of Sylvius and enlarged lateral ventricles.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.question: Which condition presents with an early prominent gait disturbance without cortical atrophy?
items.explanation: NPH presents with an early prominent gait disturbance without cortical atrophy.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging finding is more indicative of Alzheimer's disease rather than normal pressure hydrocephalus?
options: Absence of cortical atrophy on CT or MRI
items.explanation: Hippocampal atrophy on MRI favors Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.question: What is a common symptom of intracranial hypotension?
options: Seizures with degeneration of the corpus callosum
Memory loss due to thiamine deficiency
items.explanation: Intracranial hypotension presents with headache, often exacerbated by coughing.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.question: What is a known complication of ventricular shunting in patients with normal pressure hydrocephalus?
items.explanation: Subdural hematoma is a known complication of ventricular shunting.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is associated with dementia and seizures due to degeneration of the corpus callosum?
items.explanation: Marchiafava-Bignami disease is associated with dementia and seizures due to degeneration of the corpus callosum.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a typical cause of vitamin B12 deficiency?
items.explanation: Use of histamine blockers can lead to vitamin B12 deficiency.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.question: Which condition is associated with a skin rash over sun-exposed areas, glossitis, and angular stomatitis?
options: Pellagra due to nicotinic acid deficiency
items.explanation: Deficiency of nicotinic acid (pellagra) is associated with these symptoms.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for vitamin B12 deficiency due to pernicious anemia?
options: Parenteral vitamin B12 (1000 mcg intramuscularly) initially and monthly thereafter
items.explanation: Treatment with parenteral vitamin B12 is recommended for pernicious anemia.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.question: Which autoimmune syndrome can be associated with occult carcinoma and presents as dementia?
items.explanation: Limbic encephalitis is an autoimmune syndrome associated with occult carcinoma.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.question: Which imaging study can show multifocal stenoses involving medium-caliber vessels in CNS granulomatous angiitis?
items.explanation: Cerebral angiography can show multifocal stenoses involving medium-caliber vessels in CNS granulomatous angiitis.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.question: What is a common clinical feature of chronic lead poisoning?
options: Gray lead lines in the gums
items.explanation: Gray lead lines appear in the gums as a common clinical feature of chronic lead poisoning.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.question: Which condition is associated with tau-immunoreactive neurofibrillary tangles more prominent than amyloid plaques?
items.explanation: Chronic traumatic encephalopathy is associated with tau-immunoreactive neurofibrillary tangles more prominent than amyloid plaques.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.question: What treatment is used for chronic mercury poisoning?
items.explanation: Chronic mercury poisoning is treated with chelation therapy using dimercaprol (BAL).
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.statement: Carrying two ε4 alleles increases the risk for Alzheimer's disease by 10- to 15-fold in both sexes.
items.explanation: Carrying two ε4 alleles increases the risk 10- to 15-fold in both sexes.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.statement: The total U.S. health care costs related to dementia care are estimated at $200 billion in 2024.
items.explanation: Total U.S. health care costs related to dementia care are estimated at $360 billion in 2024.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.statement: Preclinical AD refers to a person with biomarker evidence of amyloid pathology in the absence of symptoms.
items.explanation: Preclinical AD involves biomarker evidence of amyloid pathology without symptoms.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Patients with Alzheimer's Disease typically show a high-amplitude, low-frequency tremor at rest.
items.explanation: AD patients often look parkinsonian but rarely have a high-amplitude, low-frequency tremor at rest.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Amyloid PET ligands bind only to neuritic Aβ plaques in Alzheimer's Disease.
items.explanation: Amyloid PET ligands also bind to vascular Aβ deposits in cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: A positive amyloid PET scan confirms the diagnosis of Alzheimer's Disease.
items.explanation: Dementia due to a non-AD disorder can be the underlying etiology in a patient who tests positively on amyloid PET.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Increases in total tau levels are specific to Alzheimer's disease.
items.explanation: Increases in total tau levels represent a nonspecific finding seen in AD and other neurodegenerative diseases.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: Plasma biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease can be affected by medical comorbidities.
items.explanation: Concentrations of Aβ and p-Tau in blood can be impacted by medical comorbidities.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: A positive family history of dementia always indicates autosomal dominant inheritance.
items.explanation: Autosomal dominant inheritance in Alzheimer's disease occurs only in 1–2% of patients.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Environmental factors such as aluminum and mercury have been conclusively proven to cause Alzheimer's disease.
items.explanation: Rigorous studies have failed to demonstrate a significant role for environmental exposures like aluminum or mercury in causing Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Amyloid precursor protein (APP) is cleaved by α and β secretases to produce Aβ peptides.
items.explanation: APP is cleaved by β and γ secretases, not α and β.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Reduction of acetylcholine in AD reflects degeneration of cholinergic neurons in the nucleus basalis of Meynert.
items.explanation: Acetylcholine reduction is due to degeneration of cholinergic neurons in the nucleus basalis of Meynert.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Mutations in presenilin genes can lead to late-age-of-onset AD.
items.explanation: Mutations in the presenilins rarely lead to late-age-of-onset AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with mutations in presenilin genes have elevated CSF levels of Aβ42 prior to symptom onset.
items.explanation: Prior to symptom onset, patients with mutations in the presenilin genes have elevated CSF levels of Aβ42.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: The Apoε4 allele is both necessary and sufficient to cause Alzheimer's disease.
items.explanation: Apoε4 is neither necessary nor sufficient to cause AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Genome-wide association studies have identified more than 40 additional genetic variants that individually impact the risk of Alzheimer's disease.
items.explanation: GWAS have identified >40 common genetic variants with small individual impacts on AD risk.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Cholinesterase inhibitors are approved for use in mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
items.explanation: Neither cholinesterase inhibitors nor memantine have proven efficacious in patients with MCI.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | NEUROTRANSMITTER-BASED THERAPIES

items.statement: Memantine is used to treat delusions and hallucinations in Alzheimer's disease.
items.explanation: Cholinesterase inhibitors may be effective in treating delusions and hallucinations, not memantine.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | NEUROTRANSMITTER-BASED THERAPIES

items.statement: Aducanumab was removed from clinical use due to ambiguous evidence of clinical efficacy.
items.explanation: Aducanumab was removed from clinical use because its evidence of clinical efficacy was ambiguous.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.statement: Antiamyloid antibody therapy is currently approved for patients with advanced stages of Alzheimer's disease.
items.explanation: Antiamyloid antibody therapy is restricted to patients with MCI or mild dementia and biomarker confirmation.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.statement: Patients on anticoagulants can be safely treated with anti-$\mathrm{A}\beta$ monoclonal antibodies.
items.explanation: Expert recommendations suggest excluding patients on anticoagulants from treatment until more safety data are available.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 2)

items.statement: ApoE $\varepsilon4$ homozygous patients are universally prohibited from receiving antiamyloid antibody therapy.
items.explanation: Several institutions prohibit or carefully restrict use in ApoE $\varepsilon4$ homozygous patients, but some may allow it after careful consideration.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 2)

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.statement: Estrogen hormone replacement therapy has been proven effective in preventing Alzheimer's disease through randomized, placebo-controlled trials.
items.explanation: Epidemiologic or observational studies suggested a decreased risk of AD with estrogen hormone replacement therapy, but it did not show efficacy in prospective, randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trials.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.statement: Antipsychotics carry a black box FDA warning for use in elderly patients with dementia.
items.explanation: Antipsychotics do indeed carry a black box FDA warning for use in elderly patients with dementia.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.statement: The effect of worsening cognition with $\beta$ secretase inhibitors was permanent.
items.explanation: The cognitive worsening observed with $\beta$ secretase inhibitors proved transient after discontinuing the drug.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES

items.statement: Monoclonal antibodies targeting phosphorylated tau have shown clinical efficacy in human studies.
items.explanation: While safety profiles for monoclonal antibodies against phosphorylated tau have been favorable, clinical results have been lacking.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES

items.statement: Memory impairment is a hallmark of early-stage Huntington's disease.
items.explanation: In Huntington's disease, memory is frequently not impaired until late in the disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.statement: NPH can be distinguished from other dementias by the presence of cortical atrophy on neuroimaging.
items.explanation: NPH is characterized by enlarged lateral ventricles with little or no cortical atrophy.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.statement: Prompt administration of thiamine can reverse Wernicke's encephalopathy if given early.
items.explanation: Thiamine administration within the first days of symptom onset may reverse Wernicke's encephalopathy.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.statement: Korsakoff's syndrome can be reversed with specific treatment.
items.explanation: There is no specific treatment for Korsakoff's syndrome as the damage from thiamine deficiency is irreversible.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.statement: Isolated folate deficiency has been proved as a specific cause of dementia.
items.explanation: Isolated folate deficiency has not been proved as a specific cause of dementia.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.statement: Primary and metastatic neoplasms of the CNS usually produce focal neurologic findings rather than dementia.
items.explanation: Neoplasms typically present with focal neurologic symptoms, not primarily dementia.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.statement: Cerebral angiography in CNS granulomatous angiitis always shows specific abnormalities.
items.explanation: The angiographic appearance is not specific and may be mimicked by other causes of vascular disease.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.statement: Chronic subdural hematoma can sometimes cause dementia, especially with underlying cortical atrophy.
items.explanation: Chronic subdural hematoma is occasionally associated with dementia, often in the context of underlying cortical atrophy.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.assertion: The identification of causative mutations and susceptibility genes for Alzheimer's disease has facilitated rapid progress in understanding its biological basis.
items.reason: Rapid progress in the development of biomarkers has enabled early detection and intervention with biologically specific therapies.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · Alzheimer's Disease | ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.assertion: The typical duration of symptomatic Alzheimer's Disease is 8–10 years.
items.reason: Some patients with AD show a steady decline in function while others have prolonged plateaus without major deterioration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the typical duration.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: In early stages of Alzheimer's Disease, language becomes impaired.
items.reason: Language impairment is a characteristic feature in the middle stages of AD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; language impairment occurs in the middle stages.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Amyloid PET imaging is useful for establishing eligibility for novel therapies.
items.reason: A positive PET scan can establish eligibility for amyloid-lowering therapies in patients meeting clinical criteria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: EEG is diagnostic for Alzheimer's Disease.
items.reason: EEG usually shows specific patterns in AD patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; EEG is usually normal or shows nonspecific slowing and is not diagnostic for AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Positive AD imaging or fluid biomarkers are highly predictive of underlying AD neuropathology.
items.reason: AD represents a primary cause to an individual patient's clinical presentation in all cases.
items.explanation: While positive biomarkers indicate AD, the primary cause of cognitive decline may not always be AD.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.assertion: The Revised Criteria for Diagnosis and Staging of AD considers amyloid PET as a core 1 biomarker.
items.reason: Amyloid PET is necessary and sufficient for the diagnosis of AD, irrespective of clinical symptoms.
items.explanation: Amyloid PET is considered a core 1 biomarker in the Revised Criteria.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.assertion: The Apoε4 allele is associated with an earlier age of onset for Alzheimer's disease.
items.reason: Each Apoε4 allele is associated with an approximately 10-year earlier age of onset.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: The Apoε4 allele is associated with a decreased risk of Alzheimer's disease in East Asians.
items.reason: East Asians have an increased risk of AD when carrying the Apoε4 allele.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; East Asians actually have an increased risk.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with low-medium tau PET at trial entry showed the greatest clinical benefit from donanemab.
items.reason: Amyloid lowering may particularly benefit patients in earlier stages of tau spread.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.assertion: Severe symptoms of ARIA include headache and dizziness.
items.reason: ARIA symptoms are typically mild and nonspecific.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; severe symptoms include seizures, stroke-like episodes, malignant hypertension, and rarely death.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · THERAPIES TARGETING AMYLOID- $ \beta $ (part 1)

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.assertion: Respite breaks are recommended to help prevent caregiver burnout.
items.reason: Caregiver 'burnout' is common and can lead to nursing home placement of the patient or new health problems for the caregiver.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why respite breaks help prevent caregiver burnout.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES
items.assertion: Vitamin E has been proven to be effective in preventing Alzheimer's disease.
items.reason: Several commonly used medications and supplements appeared associated with a decreased risk of AD in epidemiologic studies but did not show efficacy in controlled trials.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Vitamin E did not show efficacy in prospective, randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trials.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · PATIENT AND CAREGIVER EDUCATION | ADDITIONAL THERAPIES

items.assertion: CJD can be mistaken for other rapidly progressive dementias.
items.reason: CBD and DLB are more likely to be mistaken for CJD due to rapid degenerative dementia with movement abnormalities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why CJD can be mistaken for other conditions.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.assertion: NPH is caused by aqueductal stenosis.
items.reason: NPH involves a patent aqueduct of Sylvius, unlike aqueductal stenosis where the aqueduct is small.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because NPH is not caused by aqueductal stenosis; it involves a patent aqueduct.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 1)

items.assertion: A transient improvement in gait or cognition may follow lumbar puncture.
items.reason: This finding has been consistently predictive of postshunt improvement.
items.explanation: While a transient improvement can occur, it is not consistently predictive of postshunt improvement.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.assertion: Intracranial hypotension can be caused by CSF leaks.
items.reason: It presents with symptoms such as headache and dizziness, which are exacerbated by coughing or Valsalva maneuvers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 2)

items.assertion: Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause a spinal cord syndrome.
items.reason: It damages myelinated axons and affects the posterior columns and corticospinal tracts.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.assertion: Hepatic encephalopathy may progress to coma.
items.reason: It is caused by excessive vitamin B12 intake.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; hepatic encephalopathy is related to liver dysfunction.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 3)

items.assertion: Transient global amnesia results in permanent memory loss.
items.reason: The ability to form new memories returns after a period of hours, and the individual returns to normal with no recall for the period of the attack.
items.explanation: Transient global amnesia does not result in permanent memory loss; it is characterized by temporary severe episodic memory deficits.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)

items.assertion: Chronic traumatic encephalopathy can develop into full-blown dementia.
items.reason: Symptoms progress from personality changes to memory loss, often associated with parkinsonian signs and ataxia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why chronic traumatic encephalopathy can develop into full-blown dementia.
items.reference: Alzheimer s Disease · OTHER CAUSES OF DEMENTIA (part 4)
